title: The Elimination Program Failed. The Census Confirms It. The First Nations Are Here.
teaser: The United States government executed a documented, multi-century legal and military campaign designed to eliminate Indigenous peoples from the North American continent. The 2020 Census records 9.7 million people who identify as American Indian or Alaska Native. The record documents both the program and its outcome.
verifiedSource: U.S. Census Bureau (1900, 2020); Library of Congress Primary Source Archive; National Native American Boarding School Healing Coalition; Bureau of Indian Affairs; U.S. Supreme Court Records
body:
This article does not offer tribute. It offers documentation. The primary source record of what was done to Indigenous peoples in North America is extensive, legally explicit, and verifiable in federal archives. So is the record of what survived. Both are presented here without editorialization. The reader may draw conclusions. The record does not require assistance.

The Population Record: The Numbers That Frame Everything

Historians and demographers estimate that between 5 million and 15 million Indigenous people inhabited North America before European contact. The methodologies vary. The range reflects genuine scholarly debate. Every methodology places the figure in the millions.

By the 1900 United States Census, approximately 237,196 American Indians were recorded. That is the documented low point. The decline from millions to 237,000 is the demographic record of what occurred between first European contact and the beginning of the twentieth century.

The 2020 United States Census records 3.7 million people identifying as American Indian or Alaska Native alone, and 9.7 million identifying as American Indian or Alaska Native alone or in combination. That is not a rounding error. It is the largest Indigenous population count in United States census history.

From 237,196 in 1900 to 9.7 million in 2020. The reader may perform the arithmetic.

The Documented Elimination Framework

The reduction from millions to 237,196 did not occur without government architecture. The primary source record of that architecture is held in the Library of Congress, the National Archives, and federal court records.

On May 28, 1830, President Andrew Jackson signed the the Indian Removal Act. The law authorized the forced relocation of Native nations from their ancestral lands east of the Mississippi River. The Cherokee nation challenged the removal in federal court. In Worcester v. Georgia (1832), Chief Justice John Marshall ruled in their favor. President Jackson declined to enforce the ruling. The removal proceeded. The forced march that followed killed an estimated 4,000 of the 16,000 Cherokee who were relocated. The Cherokee called it Nunna daul Tsuny. The United States called it the Trail of Tears. Both names refer to the same documented event.

In 1887, Congress passed the General Allotment Act, known as the Dawes Act. The law dissolved collectively held tribal lands into individual parcels. Land remaining after allotment was declared surplus and opened to white settlement. Between 1887 and 1934, Indigenous nations lost approximately 90 million acres. That figure comes from federal government accounting. It is not disputed.

Beginning in the 1870s, the federal government funded a network of Indian boarding schools operating on an explicit policy of cultural elimination. The founding superintendent of the Carlisle Indian Industrial School, Richard Henry Pratt, documented the mission in his own words in 1892: "Kill the Indian in him, and save the man." That quotation is in the primary source record. The federal government operated or funded more than 400 such schools across 37 states between the 1870s and 1970s. The 2022 Federal Indian Boarding School Initiative Investigative Report, produced by the Department of the Interior, confirmed the deaths of at least 53 children at these institutions, with the full count still under investigation.

What the Legal Record Shows Survived

The Bureau of Indian Affairs currently recognizes 574 federally recognized tribal nations in the United States. Each holds a government-to-government relationship with the United States. Each exercises sovereign governance. The nations that were targeted by the Indian Removal Act, the Dawes Act, and the boarding school system did not disappear. They are in the federal register.

In 2020, the United States Supreme Court decided McGirt v. Oklahoma. The Court ruled, five to four, that the Muscogee (Creek) Nation's reservation in eastern Oklahoma had never been legally disestablished by Congress. Nearly half of the state of Oklahoma is reservation land under federal law. The Muscogee Nation did not litigate this into existence in 2020. The reservation existed continuously. The Court confirmed it. The United States government had simply proceeded for over a century as if it did not.

At least 150 Indigenous languages in North America are currently the subject of active revitalization efforts by tribal nations, including full-immersion schools, university programs, and digital preservation projects. Languages that were beaten out of children in federal boarding schools are being taught to grandchildren in tribally operated schools. The documentation of both facts exists simultaneously in the public record.

The Record, Complete

The United States government designed and executed a documented multi-century program to eliminate Indigenous peoples from the North American continent through forced removal, land dissolution, military force, and cultural suppression. The architecture of that program is in federal law, congressional records, and Supreme Court opinions. None of it is contested. All of it is primary source.

The 2020 Census records 9.7 million people who identify as American Indian or Alaska Native. Five hundred and seventy-four sovereign nations hold government-to-government relationships with the United States. Languages suppressed by federal policy are being taught in tribally operated schools. The Muscogee reservation that the United States government proceeded to ignore for a century was confirmed by the Supreme Court to still exist.

The program was documented. The survival is documented. The record holds both, without contradiction, and without the need for a single adjective.

The First Nations are here. The census confirms it.
